topic: High-Level Synthesis with Ant Colony Optimization Scheduling

entries.objective: I just downloaded the Kopuri & Mansouri 2004 paper and I couldn't parse the garbled OCR equations. I wanted to understand what FDS actually does before trying to understand the modification they propose.
entries.prompt: can you explain Force-Directed Scheduling in HLS - like the basic idea and why the self-force equation makes sense intuitively? I'm trying to understand what q_k(l) physically represents before I read this paper about adding ACO to it
entries.ai_output_summary: Claude explained that q_k(l) is the expected number of type-k operations concurrently active at time-step l, computed as a sum of uniform probabilities over each unscheduled op's time frame. The self-force S_il measures how much scheduling op i at time l would push the concurrency above the average -- positive self-force means it increases the peak, negative means it flattens it. The goal is to pick the assignment with the lowest total force to spread load evenly.

actions: Cross-checked the q_k definition against Eq. 1 on page V-258 of the paper image.
Confirmed that p_i(m) = 1/(alap - asap + 1) is the uniform distribution over the time frame.
The Kronecker delta interpretation (point mass at l when scheduled) matched visually.

entries.reflection: This interaction gave me a clean mental model before reading the actual equations. The AI got the substance right but I still had to go to the paper page image to confirm the exact form -- the OCR text had garbled subscripts.

entries.objective: I needed to transcribe Equation 5 (the modified force) from the paper page image. The denominator looked like a square root of a sum but I wasn't sure if it was an RMS normalisation or something else.
entries.prompt: Looking at the paper's equation 5 it shows F_il = alpha * S_il / (something under a square root) - beta * M_il - gamma * G_il. The thing under the square root looks like (1/N*lambda) * sum of all S_ij. Is that an RMS normalisation?
entries.ai_output_summary: Claude confirmed that sqrt((1/(N*lambda)) * sum S_ij^2) would be a standard RMS, but from the image description I gave, the paper appears to use sqrt((1/N*lambda) * sum S_ij) without squaring -- making it a scaled sum, not a true RMS. Claude said this is an unusual normalisation and suggested I verify the exponent directly from the image.

actions: Zoomed into page 3 image at the denominator of Eq. 5.
The sum contains S_ij without a squared exponent -- it is sum of S_ij not sum of S_ij^2.
This means the denominator is sqrt((1/N*lambda) * sum_all S_ij), which is not a true RMS.
Claude's initial framing as RMS was therefore imprecise -- the formula is printed as given.

issues: AI framed the denominator as RMS but the paper does not square S_ij inside the sum.
The word 'RMS-type' is my own interpretation; the paper just calls it the normalised self-force.

entries.reflection: The AI was useful for prompting me to check the exponent, but I had to go back to the image to settle the question. The transcription I trust now is from direct visual inspection of the page, not from the AI. This is an example where the AI was a good thinking partner but not a reliable source for fine equation details.

entries.objective: After transcribing all five equations I noticed something odd: the trail update in Eq. 3 subtracts cost(s^o) from cost(s), which gives a negative number when s is better. But Eq. 5 subtracts the trail term. I wanted to check if this creates an anti-reinforcement effect.
entries.prompt: ok so in equation 3 the trail update is M_ij += (cost(s) - cost(s^o)) / cost(s^o). If s is better, cost(s) < cost(s^o) so the increment is negative. Then in eq 5 we subtract beta*M_il. So a more negative M_il means we ADD a bigger thing to F_il which makes the force HIGHER. But A_FDS picks minimum force... so improved decisions get higher force = less likely to be picked again? did I get that wrong?
entries.ai_output_summary: Claude confirmed the reasoning: yes, as printed the equations anti-reinforce improved decisions. It suggested two possible corrections: negate the increment in Eq. 3, or change the sign in Eq. 5 to +beta*M. It also noted that the benchmarks showing improvement despite this could be explained by the self-force term dominating, with trail terms acting as a weak diversification mechanism.

actions: Traced numerical example: cost(s^o)=1000, cost(s)=900 -> increment = -0.10.
With beta=2: contribution to F_il is -2*(-0.10) = +0.20, raising the force.
Confirmed: A_FDS selects minimum force, so higher force = less likely selected.
Re-read the prose on page V-259: 'trail is positive or negative depending on whether improved results are obtained.' Mathematically it is always negative for improvement.
The inconsistency is real and verified numerically and by close reading.

issues: Paper prose and equations are inconsistent regarding sign convention.
No source code available to verify actual implementation intent.

integration.section: Section 4 (Sign-Convention Analysis) in M4; Open Questions in M1

entries.reflection: This was the most significant reasoning episode of M1. The AI confirmed my suspicion rather than generating it -- I had noticed the sign issue myself from reading the equations, and used the AI as a sanity check. The numerical trace I did by hand was the real validation. The AI's suggestion that diversification might explain the benchmark results is a reasonable hypothesis I included in M3 and M4.
